---
title: "Data Classification in Mapping"
description: "A StoryMap exploring how data classification choices affect map interpretation, visual patterns, and cartographic communication."
date: 2021-01-01
categories: [cartography, StoryMaps, data classification, geovisualization, map interpretation, geospatial communication]
image: ../../assets/images/thumbnails/data-classification-in-mapping.webp
---

The **Data Classification in Mapping** StoryMap explores how classification choices affect the way mapped data are interpreted.

This project was created as an independent geospatial communication example to examine how different classification methods can emphasize, obscure, or reshape spatial patterns. It highlights an important cartographic idea: maps are not neutral displays of data. Design decisions influence what viewers notice and how they understand geographic patterns.

## Project Role

My role included developing the StoryMap, designing the examples, organizing the explanation, and using web-based geospatial storytelling to communicate a core cartographic concept.

## Geospatial Focus

This project emphasizes:

- data classification,
- choropleth mapping,
- cartographic decision-making,
- visual interpretation,
- geospatial communication,
- the relationship between data, design, and audience understanding.

## Communication Goals

The StoryMap was designed to help viewers understand that classification methods are not just technical settings. They are interpretive choices that can change the apparent meaning of a map.

By comparing classification approaches, the project encourages viewers to think critically about how maps represent data and how cartographic choices shape geographic understanding.

## Finished Product

- [Data Classification in Mapping](https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/c52d1948f04d40209fab1605ce3c8ab9)

## Portfolio Significance

This project is a strong example of my interest in cartographic communication and geovisualization. It demonstrates how StoryMaps can be used to explain technical mapping concepts in a public-facing, accessible format.
